Hey Product Hunt! 👋  
  
I'm Steven Cen, and today we're launching ClawTeams — an AI team platform  
built specifically for e-commerce operators.  
  
The frustration that led to this: we kept seeing smart sellers use AI tools and still  
end up doing all the coordination work themselves. They had AI assistants — but they  
still had to be the manager. That's exhausting.  
  
So we built ClawTeams around a different idea:  
→ You set the goal. The AI Team Lead manages the rest.

Get 800 bonus credits ($8 value) with your first top-up of any amount. No minimum required.

We'd love your feedback — especially from sellers who've tried other AI tools and hit  
walls. What made you give up on them? What would make an AI team actually useful? [...] Short answer: it's a two-step process, not either/or.

When the Team Lead agent hits something like an expired token from a connected store, it first tries safe, reversible self-healing steps — retrying the auth flow, refreshing via the stored refresh token if available, or falling back to a cached state so nothing downstream breaks silently. If that resolves it, you just see a quiet log entry, no interruption. [...] Overview

results.title: ClawTeam Agent Swarm Intelligence
results.content: It feels like you're trying to manage a team of brilliant interns who just refuse to talk to each other. It is total chaos. And this is the exact frustration that agent swarm intelligence is built to fix. And here is the big idea, the solution. We're making an evolutionary leap, moving away from that single lonely robot and into the idea of a swarm. It's where these individual agents can band together and become something so much more powerful than the sum of their parts. So, what is agent swarm intelligence really? Well, simply put, it's a system where AI agents can actually organize themselves. They can look at a big complicated goal, figure out how to break it down into smaller tasks, assign those tasks to the right team members, and then talk to each other the whole time to bring it [...] Codex are amazing. They're absolute powerhouses. But as the source material points out, they've got this one critical flaw. They're lone wolves. They're designed to work completely by themselves. And what happens when you have a really complex project? Well, you, the human, end up becoming the bottleneck. You're the one stuck in the middle trying to play coordinator, copying code from one agent, pasting it for another, managing all the different contexts. You're basically trying to stitch everything together by hand. It's slow, it's messy, and it just doesn't scale. Seriously, think about it. Have you ever tried to get one AI to handle your front end, another one for the back end, and maybe a third to deal with the database? It feels like you're trying to manage a team of brilliant [...] time, in parallel, and you'll never, ever get one of those dreaded merge conflicts. Okay, so they've each got their own little workshop, but how do they actually talk to each other and coordinate their work? Do they need some fancy API or a special messaging platform? Nope. The answer is much, much simpler than that. This right here, this is the secret sauce. When a new agent joins a Claude team, its very first prompt gets this little piece of text automatically added. A text that teaches it a few simple commands for teamwork. It instantly learns how to send messages, check its inbox, and report on its progress. And because of this, literally any agent that can run in the command line can become a team player instantly with zero changes to its own code. All right, so that's all the

results.title: I Loved My OpenClaw AI Agent—Until It Turned on Me
results.content:
It is important to note that giving OpenClaw complete access to your real email is incredibly risky, because AI models can be tricked into sharing private information with an attacker. I set up an elaborate email-forwarding, read-only scheme, but even that is probably too dangerous, so I deactivated it after testing. Having OpenClaw access email, Slack, and other channels also involves a lot of technical steps, and it can be quite frustrating (I had several dummy Gmail accounts suspended 🙄).

## Negotiation
